name: Role
summary: A database identity that defines access permissions and can represent a user or a group.
description: A role is a database-level identity that controls who can do what. Roles can represent individual users (with login capability) or groups of permissions (without login). You can grant privileges to roles and then assign roles to other roles, creating a flexible permission hierarchy. Roles are the foundation of database security.

postgresql.notes: In PostgreSQL, users and groups are both roles — the difference is whether LOGIN is granted.

mysql.notes: Roles were added in MySQL 8.0. Users and roles are separate concepts.

sqlite.notes: SQLite has no user/role system. Access control must be handled at the application or filesystem level.

tips: Follow the principle of least privilege — grant only the minimum permissions needed for each role.
Use group roles to manage permissions centrally, then assign users to groups instead of granting permissions individually.
In MySQL, roles must be activated with SET DEFAULT ROLE — otherwise the user won't inherit the role's privileges.

name: Row-Level Security (RLS)
summary: Restricts which rows a user can see or modify based on security policies.
description: Row-Level Security (RLS) allows you to control access to individual rows in a table based on the user or role executing the query. When RLS is enabled, even a SELECT * query will only return rows that the user is allowed to see. This is powerful for multi-tenant applications where different customers share the same table but should only see their own data.

mysql.notes: MySQL does not support RLS. Implement row-level filtering in application code or using views.

mariadb.notes: MariaDB does not support RLS. Use views or application-level filtering.

tips: RLS policies are enforced automatically — even if application code has a bug, the database still protects the data.
Remember that table owners bypass RLS by default. Use ALTER TABLE ... FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY to apply policies to owners too.
Test RLS policies thoroughly — a misconfigured policy can either leak data or block legitimate access.

name: Policy
summary: A rule that defines which rows a role can access under Row-Level Security.
description: A policy is a named rule attached to a table that defines which rows specific roles can SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE when Row-Level Security is enabled. Policies use USING clauses (for reads) and WITH CHECK clauses (for writes) to filter rows. Multiple policies can exist on a single table, and they are combined using OR (permissive) or AND (restrictive) logic.

syntax.postgresql:
-- Permissive policy (default): users see their own data
CREATE POLICY own_data_policy ON orders
  FOR ALL
  TO app_user
  USING (user_id = current_setting('app.current_user_id')::INT)
  WITH CHECK (user_id = current_setting('app.current_user_id')::INT);

-- Restrictive policy: additionally require active status
CREATE POLICY active_only_policy ON orders
  AS RESTRICTIVE
  FOR SELECT
  TO app_user
  USING (status != 'cancelled');

-- Admin policy: admins can see everything
CREATE POLICY admin_policy ON orders
  FOR ALL
  TO admin_role
  USING (TRUE)
  WITH CHECK (TRUE);

-- View policies
SELECT * FROM pg_policies WHERE tablename = 'orders';

tips: USING controls which rows can be read (SELECT, UPDATE, DELETE). WITH CHECK controls which rows can be written (INSERT, UPDATE).
Permissive policies (default) are combined with OR — any matching policy grants access. Restrictive policies are combined with AND.
Always create an admin policy with USING (TRUE) so administrators can still access all data when needed.

name: GRANT / REVOKE
summary: Controls which privileges a role has on database objects.
description: GRANT and REVOKE are SQL commands that control access privileges. GRANT gives a role permission to perform specific operations (SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, etc.) on specific objects (tables, schemas, functions). REVOKE removes those permissions. This is the primary mechanism for implementing access control in a database.

sqlite.notes: SQLite does not support GRANT/REVOKE. Use filesystem permissions to control access.

tips: Use WITH GRANT OPTION sparingly — it allows the grantee to grant the same privilege to others, which can be hard to track.
In PostgreSQL, don't forget GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA — without it, users can't see objects in the schema even with table-level grants.
Review grants periodically with queries on information_schema or pg_roles to ensure no excessive privileges exist.

name: Definer vs Invoker Rights
summary: Controls whether a routine runs with the permissions of its creator or the calling user.
description: When a function or procedure runs, it can execute with the privileges of the user who created it (DEFINER/SECURITY DEFINER) or the user who is calling it (INVOKER/SECURITY INVOKER). Definer rights are like 'setuid' in Unix — the routine temporarily escalates privileges. This is useful when you want a user to perform specific actions through a function without granting direct table access.

syntax.postgresql:
-- SECURITY DEFINER: runs with creator's privileges
CREATE FUNCTION get_user_email(uid INT)
RETURNS VARCHAR
LANGUAGE SQL
SECURITY DEFINER
AS $$
  SELECT email FROM users WHERE id = uid;
$$;

-- SECURITY INVOKER (default): runs with caller's privileges
CREATE FUNCTION search_products(keyword TEXT)
RETURNS TABLE(id INT, name VARCHAR)
LANGUAGE SQL
SECURITY INVOKER
AS $$
  SELECT id, name FROM products WHERE name ILIKE '%' || keyword || '%';
$$;

postgresql.notes: Default is SECURITY INVOKER. Use SECURITY DEFINER for privilege escalation.

mysql.notes: Default is SQL SECURITY DEFINER. Views, functions, procedures, and triggers all support this.

tips: Use SECURITY DEFINER to create controlled APIs — users call the function without needing direct table access.
Be very careful with SECURITY DEFINER functions — a SQL injection vulnerability could escalate to the definer's full privileges.
In PostgreSQL, always set search_path explicitly in SECURITY DEFINER functions to prevent search path attacks.
